summary:
The Capture of Atahualpa at Cajamarca (November 15, 1532 AD) was the pivotal moment of the [[event:3626|Spanish conquest of the Inca Empire]] — an act of extraordinary military audacity and psychological calculation by Francisco Pizarro that changed the history of an entire continent. Pizarro's force of 168 soldiers, concealed in buildings surrounding Cajamarca's main plaza, waited as Atahualpa entered with a ceremonial escort of several thousand attendants, most unarmed. When the Dominican friar Vicente de Valverde presented Atahualpa with a breviary and demanded he submit to Christianity and the Spanish crown, Atahualpa reportedly examined and discarded the book; Pizarro then gave the signal for attack.

The ambush was devastating: Spanish cavalry, cannon, and armored infantry burst from their hiding places, killing thousands of Inca attendants in the confined plaza within hours. Atahualpa himself was seized alive by Pizarro — a prize worth more than any treasure, since his captivity paralyzed the Inca empire's decision-making. The ransom Atahualpa negotiated — filling a room 22 feet by 17 feet with gold to a height of 8 feet, and filling two adjacent rooms with silver — represents approximately 13,000 pounds of gold and 26,000 pounds of silver in modern estimates. Despite paying the ransom in full, Atahualpa was tried on charges of fratricide (for executing his half-brother Huascar) and idolatry, and garroted in July 1533. His baptism immediately before execution meant he was strangled rather than burned — a mercy that allowed his body to remain intact, which was important in Inca cosmology, though historians note the Spanish may not have understood this significance.

Atahualpa execution baptism garroting 1533 Inca emperor
